storm.summary_layperson: No storms anywhere near the area today — it's a clear, hot afternoon with the main concerns being an Air Quality Alert and minor tidal flooding along low-lying docks tonight.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Long Island Sound water (73°F) creates a weak stable layer over the water, but with no incoming storms, the protective mechanism isn't a factor today.
storm.detail_html:
The regional picture is benign this afternoon. KOKX radar shows no storm cells anywhere in the approach sector — the 20:13Z (4:13 PM EDT) scan returned zero reflectivity echoes above the 35 dBZ detection threshold. Nothing is within 50 miles, nothing is inbound.

Instability is negligible. HRRR places CAPE at just 40 J/kg at the current hour, dropping to zero by early evening — far below the 500 J/kg floor needed to sustain convection. The KOKX Area Forecast Discussion confirms the suppression explicitly: "this will hinder convective development." The next organized convective chance is Wednesday evening/night, when a weak cold front may interact with enough moisture to break the cap, with a broader frontal system this weekend.

The coastal stabilization mechanism is present but moot today. Sound water is 73°F versus an inland air temperature of 81°F — a 7°F differential that produces a weak marine stable layer over the water. This would normally reduce the punch-through risk for incoming storm cells, but with nothing inbound, it is not a factor. The protective mechanism threshold (10°F delta with a confirmed storm threat) is not met.

Three active NWS alerts — none storm-related. The Air Quality Alert runs through tomorrow evening (high ozone and particulate levels; the hazy afternoon sky is the tell). The Heat Advisory begins tomorrow at 11 AM and runs through Tuesday evening. Tonight, a minor Coastal Flood Statement takes effect at 9 PM EDT through 1 AM: minor low-lying flooding from astronomical high tides combined with the persistent SSW onshore push — nothing severe, but worth checking dock lines and leaving extra scope.

Bottom line: no thunderstorm or severe weather risk today. The next organized convective threat is at least two days out. Enjoy the evening sail.

hourly.shift_note: steady SSW, peak of race window
steady S, thermal relaxing near sunset

wind.tactics_blurb: Steady S–SSW at 11–12 kt throughout the window with pressure fairly uniform across the cove — slight advantage on the right (east) side near the mouth. Start mid-line, lean right on the first beat.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime A+ confirmed — thermal with synoptic tailwind. The deltaT of 7.2°F (inland 81°F vs Sound water 73°F) clears the 5°F thermal ingredient gate, and the synoptic gradient is already pushing S/SSW at 10–13 kt: KHPN (inland Westchester) 8 kt S, KISP (Islip) 13 kt SSW, RYC 10.1 kt SSW, KBID (Block Island) 16 kt SSW. NWS forecasting south 14 mph. Gradient and thermal are pointing the same direction and adding — the best-racing A+ flavor: reliable pressure, steady direction, no collapse risk through the race window.

HRRR and the western-LIS station ensemble are tightly aligned. The model/obs divergence is just 3° (HRRR at 195°, station median at 198° SSW) — no maturation correction needed, and the deltaT of 7.2°F sits just below the 8°F correction gate anyway. HRRR directions are used directly for the evening hours. RYC ground truth: SSW 200°, 10.1 kt mean, 11.4 kt 10-min average, 15.2 kt gust. The fleet races in the 11–12 kt bracket with gusts to 13–15 kt — powered up, great conditions for most one-design classes.

Cove geography for SSW: flow enters cleanly up the cove's N–S axis. Pressure is fairly uniform with a slight advantage on the right (east) side near the mouth, where sheltering from the west shore is reduced. The call is subtle — a "lean right" tiebreaker, not a dramatic pin-end situation. Direction holds in a tight 190–196° window through the entire forecast period; oscillations of ±5–8° are realistic. No big persistent shifts expected.

Post-sunset behavior on A+: sunset is approximately 8:16 PM EDT. On a pure-thermal day this marks the collapse window, but the synoptic S/SSW gradient persists independently of the sun. Expect a gentle speed taper from ~12 kt to ~11 kt near sunset as the thermal component eases — no directional change, no abrupt drop. Wind remains fully raceable through 22:00 and beyond. The HRRR extended outlook (hours 7–12, lower confidence) shows sustained 11–12 kt with shear increasing overnight, consistent with the gradient firming into Tuesday.

Ensemble note: direction_agreement is 0.69 (moderate), and the APRS coefficient of variation is 0.86. Both are expected in this regime — sheltered suburban APRS sites show light variable winds (1–4 kt) that don't track the SSW gradient well, while the authoritative coastal stations (RYC, all coastal METARs) are unanimous. Use the METAR and RYC reads for race decisions; disregard the APRS speed means.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (WLIS) wind observation 75 minutes old — last update 19:15Z; SST value still usable
KFRG (Farmingdale LI) anemometer offline — excluded from direction and speed analysis
KWST (Westerly RI) anemometer offline — excluded from analysis
KPVD (Providence) wind direction not reported — backdoor sentinel degraded to 2 of 3 stations (not a concern in July, outside seasonal window)
